albums.title: The Bends
albums.reason: With Reckoner clocking 125 all-time plays, you clearly love Radiohead deep — but The Bends is the raw, guitar-forward side of them you may not have revisited in a while.

albums.title: Homework
albums.reason: You've played Daft Punk nearly 3000 times and your top tracks are all over their catalogue — Homework is the raw, filter-house origin story that deserves a fresh spin.

albums.title: Meddle
albums.reason: With Pink Floyd sitting at 1199 all-time plays and the previously recommended albums already covering their peak era, Meddle — home of Echoes — is the deep vault gem you should be revisiting.

albums.title: Led Zeppelin IV
albums.reason: You've played Led Zeppelin 964 times and Physical Graffiti was already surfaced — IV is the bedrock classic with the full emotional range that earns repeat listens for life.

albums.title: Humbug
albums.reason: 809 all-time plays shows how deep your Arctic Monkeys love runs — Humbug is their most underplayed gem, a dusty psychedelic pivot that rewards returning ears.

albums.title: Junior
albums.reason: Röyksopp has 1062 all-time plays and Melody A.M. and The Understanding have already come up — Junior is the bright, synth-pop counterpart that deserves to come back into rotation.

albums.title: Audio, Video, Disco
albums.reason: Justice sits at 771 all-time plays and you know D.A.N.C.E. and Phantom well — Audio, Video, Disco is their underrated rock-leaning second album that's due a comeback in your queue.

albums.title: (What's the Story) Morning Glory?
albums.reason: Oasis has 617 all-time plays and this is the cornerstone record — if you haven't played it front-to-back recently, it's comfort food at its most reliable.

albums.title: Ibifornia
albums.reason: Your 2940 Daft Punk plays and love of French electronic music make this a natural fit — Cassius were peers of that whole Ed Banger / Versailles house scene and Ibifornia is their sun-drenched late-career masterpiece.

albums.title: Machine Says Yes
albums.reason: You love Leftfield, Underworld and Orbital — FC Kahuna occupied the same late-90s/early-00s UK electronic space and Machine Says Yes is a cult classic you've almost certainly overlooked.

albums.title: The Mirror Conspiracy
albums.reason: Your deep love of Air and Röyksopp's atmospheric side maps perfectly onto Thievery Corporation's lush, globally-inflected downtempo — The Mirror Conspiracy is their richest, most transportive record.

albums.title: Business Casual
albums.reason: Your 771 Justice plays and overall French electro affinity suggests you'd love Chromeo's silky, Prince-influenced electro-funk — Business Casual is their most polished and fun record.

albums.title: Man with a Movie Camera
albums.reason: You love James Blake's emotional depth and Orbital's layered electronics — The Cinematic Orchestra's reimagining of a 1929 silent film is a stunning jazz-meets-electronica record that belongs in your collection.

albums.title: Unrest
albums.reason: Your love of Röyksopp's quieter, melodic side (and Air) points straight to Erlend Øye's solo debut — a hushed, heartfelt set of electronic bedroom pop that feels like it was made for late nights.

albums.title: Not for Threes
albums.reason: With Orbital and Underworld in your top 20 all-time, Plaid are a criminally overlooked peer — Not for Threes is their warmest, most melodically alive record and a clear gap in your electronic canon.

albums.title: Londinium
albums.reason: You have Radiohead, James Blake and Underworld at the core of your taste — Archive's debut is a brooding, orchestral trip-hop masterpiece that sits in exactly that emotional territory.

albums.title: Source
albums.reason: Your recent listens to Sault, Charles Bradley and soul-adjacent jazz suggest you're drawn to music with deep roots and forward energy — Nubya Garcia's Source is exactly that, a stunning modern jazz record with Afro-Caribbean warmth.

albums.title: The Kid
albums.reason: Your recent heavy rotation of Nils Frahm, Ólafur Arnalds and Hania Rani points to a love of expressive, emotionally textured electronic music — Kaitlyn Aurelia Smith's The Kid is a joyful, Buchla-synthesizer-led journey that fits perfectly.

albums.title: A Fable
albums.reason: Your Oscar Peterson Trio and Nils Frahm plays show a love of piano-led music with both technical depth and emotional warmth — Armenian jazz pianist Tigran Hamasyan brings a folk-inflected, genre-defying voice you'd likely love.

albums.title: Articulation
albums.reason: Your recent listening to Martin Kohlstedt, Ben Lukas Boysen and Max Cooper puts you squarely in the world of thoughtful, expressive electronic music — Rival Consoles' Articulation is a masterclass in that same space.

albums.title: Morning Matters
albums.reason: Your love of Bill Withers, Charles Bradley and Sault's soulful threads suggests Yazmin Lacey's hushed, piano-and-voice soul jazz would feel like a natural next step — Morning Matters is quietly stunning.

albums.title: Yoyogi Park
albums.reason: Your plays of Dominik Eulberg, Super Flu and Tosca show a real affinity for warm, thoughtful deep house — Lawrence's Yoyogi Park is a touchstone of the Kompakt label's emotive, melodic side.

albums.title: The Plateaux of Mirror
albums.reason: Your recent immersion in Hania Rani, Felix Rösch and Brambles points to a love of sparse, painterly piano ambience — Harold Budd's collaboration with Brian Eno is an untouchable landmark in exactly that world.

albums.title: Reversed
albums.reason: Your recent listens to Apparat, Ólafur Arnalds and Ben Lukas Boysen suggest you're deep in the cinematic electronic world right now — Ocoeur is a French producer in that same lane whose Reversed album is quietly essential.

albums.title: Felt
albums.reason: You've been playing Nils Frahm constantly this month — Felt, recorded at midnight on a felt-muted piano to not wake his neighbours, captures exactly the quiet, close-mic intimacy you're clearly craving right now.

albums.title: Trio
albums.reason: With Joep Beving at 15 plays this month and Prehension and Solipsism already surfaced, Trio is the natural next chapter — his most expansive and emotionally open record, and it fits the reflective mood of your recent listening perfectly.

albums.title: No Hassle
albums.reason: Tosca is your most-played artist this month by a wide margin — No Hassle is their warm, shuffling mid-period record that sits between the dubbed-out Suzuki vibe you're loving and their more melodic side.

albums.title: Cascade
albums.reason: With 23 plays this month, Ben Lukas Boysen is clearly resonating deeply right now — Cascade is his most orchestrally ambitious record and a natural complement to the Gravity and Spells albums you've been in.

albums.title: Carve the Peat
albums.reason: You've been playing Erland Cooper's Holm remixes this week and he's showing up in your recent tracks — Carve the Peat is his most recent solo record, continuing the same elemental, windswept Orkney-inspired beauty.

albums.artist: A Winged Victory for the Sullen
albums.title: Invisible Cities
albums.reason: You've played four A Winged Victory for the Sullen tracks this week alone including 'So That The City Can Begin To Exist' from this very album — Invisible Cities clearly deserves a proper full listen right now.

albums.title: Mordechai
albums.reason: Khruangbin is in your monthly top artists at 12 plays and Con Todo El Mundo has been on repeat — Mordechai is their most song-forward and soulfully warm record, a perfect companion to where you are right now.

albums.title: re:member
albums.reason: You've played multiple Ólafur Arnalds tracks this week including 21:05 and Love and Glory — re:member is the album that best captures the generative, shimmering piano-meets-electronics sound you're clearly in the mood for.

albums.title: Changes
albums.reason: Neal Francis showed up at 12 plays this month and Return to Zero has been in heavy rotation — Changes is his soulful, piano-driven follow-up that doubles down on the vintage warmth and deserves to be next in line.

songs.title: Exit Music (For a Film)
songs.reason: Reckoner is your most-played Radiohead track at 125 plays, but this devastating OK Computer closer is a different emotional register entirely — one you shouldn't leave in the vault much longer.

songs.title: Dogs
songs.reason: With 1199 Pink Floyd plays and Animals already surfaced as an album, Dogs is the centrepiece track — 17 minutes of cold, relentless genius that rewards full attention when you're ready to commit.

songs.title: Voyager
songs.reason: You've played Daft Punk nearly 3000 times and your top tracks span Discovery well — but Voyager, the most purely euphoric moment on that album, deserves a standalone spin today.

songs.title: Dark & Long
songs.reason: You have 633 Underworld plays and Born Slippy is in your all-time top 20 — but Dark & Long, their glacial, 10-minute techno drift from Dubnobasswithmyheadman, is the deeper vault track that should be back in rotation.

songs.title: Mr. Brightside
songs.reason: 926 all-time Killers plays puts them firmly in your bedrock — Mr. Brightside is the track that never gets old no matter how many times you've heard it, and it's probably been a while.

songs.title: Billie Jean
songs.reason: Michael Jackson has 1852 all-time plays — Billie Jean is one of the greatest pop productions ever made and the kind of track that sounds brand new every time you give it proper headphone attention.

songs.title: Citizen Erased
songs.reason: Muse sits at 712 all-time plays and Origin of Symmetry was already surfaced — Citizen Erased is the centrepiece of that album, their most ambitious and emotionally complete track.

songs.title: Barely Legal
songs.reason: 652 Strokes plays and Is This It already surfaced as an album — Barely Legal is one of the most effortlessly cool tracks on that record and a great reminder of how loose and alive they sounded.

songs.title: Rusty Nails
songs.reason: Your love of Apparat and deep electronic music with emotional weight maps perfectly onto Moderat — Rusty Nails from their II album is the centrepiece track, a slow-burning beauty built for headphones.

songs.title: Airglow Fires
songs.reason: Your Daft Punk depth and love of melodic, euphoric electronics makes Lone a natural discovery — Airglow Fires is a shimmering, blissed-out highlight from Galaxy Garden that sits between house, IDM and pure joy.

songs.title: Panorama
songs.reason: Your love of Orbital and Leftfield's long-form electronic craft points toward Dousk's warm, hypnotic progressive house — Panorama is a subtle, beautifully constructed track that rewards patience.

songs.title: Gabriel
songs.reason: You love James Blake's emotional electronics and the soulful depth of Groove Armada — Lamb's Gabriel is a stunning piece of UK trip-hop that floats somewhere between those worlds with real grace.

songs.title: Olson
songs.reason: Your love of Radiohead's atmospheric side and melodic electronic music puts you in Boards of Canada territory — Olson from Geogaddi is their most emotionally direct and haunting short piece, a perfect entry point.

songs.title: Take Me Into Your Skin
songs.reason: With Underworld and Leftfield deep in your catalogue, Trentemøller's slow-burning, cinematic electronic work is a natural peer — Take Me Into Your Skin is his signature track, dark and completely absorbing.

songs.title: Europe, After the Rain
songs.reason: Your love of Pink Floyd's cinematic scope and James Blake's emotional weight suggest Max Richter's early work would resonate — Europe, After the Rain from Memoryhouse is a quietly devastating piece.

songs.title: Spinner
songs.reason: Your love of Orbital's layered electronics and the more atmospheric side of your catalogue makes Portico Quartet a strong fit — Spinner is an urgent, rhythmically hypnotic track that bridges jazz and electronic music beautifully.

songs.title: Penn Ar Maez
songs.reason: Your deep recent immersion in Hania Rani, Felix Rösch and Nils Frahm puts you squarely in expressive solo piano territory — Yann Tiersen's Penn Ar Maez from EUSA is a long, contemplative piece with real emotional pull.

songs.title: I Will Be Grateful for This Day
songs.reason: Your recent plays of Charles Bradley, Sault and Bill Withers show a love of music with genuine human warmth at its core — Alabaster DePlume's music sits in that same emotional territory, tender and completely unique.

songs.title: Parallel Traditions
songs.reason: Your recent listening to Brambles, Martin Kohlstedt and Four Tet suggests an appetite for music that creates its own world — Khyam Allami's microtonal oud work is unlike almost anything else and rewards deep listening.

songs.title: Caecilia
songs.reason: Your love of Apparat, Ólafur Arnalds and textured electronic music suggests Fennesz would be a revelation — Caecilia from Venice is a shimmering wall of processed guitar and pure emotional atmosphere.

songs.title: Cold Day Light
songs.reason: Your Kompakt-adjacent listens (Super Flu, Dominik Eulberg, Tosca) and love of emotional electronics make Raz Ohara a strong pick — Cold Day Light is a hushed, tender track from his debut that sits beautifully in that world.

songs.title: Glimmer
songs.reason: Your heavy recent listening to Brambles' Charcoal and Hania Rani's Ghosts suggests you love music that feels like still water — Jacaszek's Glimmer is a Polish composer working in the same fragile, luminous space.

songs.title: Glad I Tried
songs.reason: Your recent Weval, Super Flu and Tosca plays show a love of warm, melodic electronic music with a human touch — Jonny Nash's Glad I Tried is a blissful, guitar-ambient track that fits perfectly.

songs.title: Natsukashii
songs.reason: Your Oscar Peterson Trio plays and recent lean toward contemplative music suggests Nordic jazz would resonate — Helge Lien Trio's Natsukashii is a quietly gorgeous piano trio piece with real personality.

songs.title: Toilet Brushes - More
songs.reason: You've been deep in Nils Frahm this month — Toilet Brushes - More is the track that captures his live energy at its most intense and cathartic, a different texture from the quieter pieces you've been playing.

songs.artist: A Winged Victory for the Sullen
songs.title: A symphony of Lights Plays Over a Wrecked Sea
songs.reason: You've played four AWVFTS tracks this week and their self-titled and The Undivided Five are both in your recent rotation — this track from Invisible Cities is the emotional centrepiece and the right one to single out.

songs.title: Sonder
songs.reason: Joep Beving has 15 plays this month and multiple albums already surfaced — Sonder is one of his most quietly moving individual pieces, and it fits the contemplative, inward mood of your recent listening perfectly.

songs.title: Capriccio
songs.reason: With 30 Tosca plays this month, you're clearly in the mood for their sound — Capriccio is a slower, dubby deep cut that captures the meditative, late-night side of their catalogue.

songs.title: White Gloves
songs.reason: Khruangbin has 12 plays this month and you're clearly in their world — White Gloves from Con Todo El Mundo is one of their most hypnotic and soulful tracks, the kind that rewards repeat listens.

songs.title: Tern
songs.reason: Erland Cooper appeared in your most recent tracks this week — Tern, from his Solan album, is one of his most strikingly beautiful pieces, a perfect companion to the reflective, windswept mood you're in.

songs.title: Breathing
songs.reason: With 23 Ben Lukas Boysen plays this month, you're deep in his world right now — Breathing from Cascade is the track that opens up widest, a long, cinematic piece that earns its emotional payoff.

songs.title: Alameda
songs.reason: Neal Francis is in your monthly top artists and Return to Zero has been on heavy rotation — Alameda from Changes is one of his most emotionally resonant tracks, and it fits the soulful, reflective mood of your week.

songs.title: Near Light
songs.reason: You've played multiple Ólafur Arnalds tracks this week and he's clearly central to your current mood — Near Light is one of his most beloved individual pieces and a perfect standalone listen to anchor the week.

djSets.setName: HMWL Podcast 430: Niconé & David Hasert (Kompakt / Get Physical / HMWL)
Daphni - FOLD, London. Nov 22, 2025. 7hr DJ set
Klausmarie b2b Alec Troniq - RAVIN' RAVIN' 2026 @ Sektor Evolution
